subject: math
topic_title: Logic — Statements, Negation and Conditional Statements
source_title: New General Mathematics SSS 1 by M.F. Macrae et al
learning_objectives: Identify logical statements and distinguish them from non-statements
Write the negation of a given statement
Write and interpret conditional (implication) statements using p ⇒ q
Identify the converse, inverse and contrapositive of conditional statements
Apply the chain rule to test validity of arguments

sections.heading: Logical Statements
sections.content: A logical statement is a declaration (verbal or written) that is either true (T) or false (F), but not both. Questions, exclamations, commands and expressions of feeling are not logical statements. Examples of statements: 'Nigeria is an African country' (T); 'The earth is conical in shape' (F); '4 + 7 = 11' (T). Non-statements: 'Who is he?'; 'What a lovely man!'; 'Take the pencil away.'

sections.heading: Negation
sections.content: The negation of statement p is written ∼p and means 'it is not the case that p'. If p is true, ∼p is false, and vice versa. Example: if p = 'Nigeria is a rich country', then ∼p = 'Nigeria is not a rich country'. If r = '3 + 4 = 8', then ∼r = '3 + 4 ≠ 8'.

sections.heading: Conditional Statements and Implication
sections.content: The statement 'If p then q' (written p ⇒ q) is a conditional statement. p is called the antecedent and q is the consequent. Truth table for p ⇒ q: T,T→T; T,F→F; F,T→T; F,F→T. The implication is false only when the antecedent is true and the consequent is false. Converse: q ⇒ p. Inverse: ∼p ⇒ ∼q. Contrapositive: ∼q ⇒ ∼p.

sections.heading: Biconditional Statements and the Chain Rule
sections.content: The biconditional p ⟺ q means 'p if and only if q' — both p ⇒ q and q ⇒ p are true. p ⟺ q is true only when p and q have the same truth value (both T or both F). The chain rule states: if p ⇒ q and q ⇒ r, then p ⇒ r. This makes an argument valid. An argument is invalid if it does not follow this chain format.
